g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of pipelining, a fundamental technique for improving processor performance through instruction overlap.
g.text: Overlapping Instruction Execution for High Performance

g.data-narration: Pipelining is like an assembly line where different stages work concurrently, processing multiple instructions simultaneously.
g.text: A technique inspired by assembly lines where multiple instructions are in
different stages of execution simultaneously, improving throughput.

g.data-narration: Without pipelining, processors waste hardware resources since only one stage executes at a time, leading to inefficiency.
g.text: Sequential execution wastes hardware - stages sit idle while waiting for
previous instructions to complete. Pipelining keeps all stages busy!

g.data-narration: The 5-stage pipeline divides instruction execution into Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory access, and Write-back stages.
Pipeline registers between stages hold intermediate results, ensuring each stage works independently on different instructions.
g.text: Registers between stages hold data and control signals as instructions move through the pipeline.

g.data-narration: This timeline shows three instructions overlapping in the pipeline, completing in 7 cycles instead of 15 sequential cycles.
The ideal speedup equals the number of pipeline stages. For a 5-stage pipeline, maximum speedup is 5x.
Real pipelines face hazards that cause stalls. There are three main types: structural, data, and control hazards.
g.text: Hazards prevent instructions from executing in their designated cycle, causing stalls (bubbles) in the pipeline.

g.data-narration: Structural hazards occur when hardware can't support all pipeline combinations. Solution: Harvard architecture with separate I-cache and D-cache.
g.text: Example: Single memory for both instructions and data causes conflict when
LOAD is in MEM stage and next instruction needs to fetch from IF stage.

g.data-narration: Data hazards occur when instructions depend on results not yet written. Forwarding routes results directly from EX/MEM stage.
g.text: Example: ADD R1,R2,R3 then SUB R4,R1,R5 - SUB needs R1 value that ADD
writes in WB stage, but SUB needs it in EX stage.

g.data-narration: Control hazards arise from branches changing the PC. The solution is branch prediction to speculatively fill the pipeline.
g.text: Branches change PC, but outcome is known only in EX stage. Instructions
fetched after branch may be wrong and must be discarded.

g.data-narration: Each hazard type has specific solutions: separate caches for structural, forwarding for data, and branch prediction for control hazards.
Pipelining increases throughput by overlapping instructions, achieving near-ideal CPI of 1 with k-stage pipeline and k times speedup.
g.text: Pipelining increases instruction throughput, not individual
For k-stage pipeline, maximum speedup equals k.
Real speedup is lower due to hazards.

g.data-narration: Pipelining is used in all modern CPUs from smartphones to supercomputers, forming the foundation of high-performance computing.
g.text: Pipelining is the foundation of all modern processors. Modern CPUs use advanced
pipelining with 10+ stages, superscalar execution, and out-of-order processing.

g.data-narration: This concludes our pipelining module. Understanding these concepts is essential for grasping modern processor design and performance optimization.
g.text: Master pipelining to understand modern high-performance processors!
